title: GetX
objectives: Weigh GetX's convenience against its trade-offs

sections.markdown: GetX is an all-in-one package combining state management, dependency injection, and routing with minimal boilerplate. Reactive state uses .obs variables and the Obx widget rebuilds automatically.

GetX's appeal is speed of development: reactive state, DI and navigation without BuildContext, in very little code. Its criticism: it encourages tight coupling/global singletons, hides magic, and can make large apps harder to test and reason about.
Using Get.to/Get.find as global access everywhere creates hidden dependencies that hurt testability. If you use GetX, still keep logic in controllers and inject them deliberately.
GetX is great for prototypes and small apps where velocity matters. For large, team-maintained codebases, many teams prefer Riverpod/BLoC for explicitness and testability.

flashcards.front: What three things does GetX combine?
flashcards.back: State management, dependency injection, and routing.

flashcards.front: How does reactive GetX state work?
flashcards.back: Mark variables .obs and wrap UI in Obx(); it rebuilds when they change.
Encourages global singletons/tight coupling and 'magic', hurting testability at scale.

options: An all-in-one state + DI + routing package
quiz.explain: It bundles several concerns into one package.
Obx rebuilds when observed .obs values change.
Global access patterns reduce testability and clarity.

challenges.text: Build a reactive counter with GetxController + Obx.
Use GetX routing and DI (Get.put/Get.find) for two screens.
Refactor a GetX app to inject controllers explicitly for testability.
Compare a GetX feature vs the same feature in Riverpod and discuss trade-offs.

interviewQuestions: What does GetX combine and how?
How does reactive GetX state work?
What are the criticisms of GetX?
When is GetX a good fit?
